text: Solar panels convert sunlight into electricity using the photovoltaic (PV) effect. When sunlight's energy (photons) strikes a panel, it knocks electrons free from silicon atoms. An internal electric field forces these loose electrons to flow through a circuit, generating usable electricity. Union of Concerned Scientists +3The Step-by-Step Process Light Absorption: A solar panel is made up of multiple individual solar cells constructed of silicon. When sunlight hits these panels, the cells absorb photons (tiny packets of light energy).The Electric Field: Each solar cell has two layers of silicon. The top layer is treated to have a negative charge, while the bottom layer has a positive charge. This creates an electric field at the border between the two layers.Current Generation: When the photons smash into the silicon, they knock electrons loose from their atoms. The built-in electric field pushes these free electrons in one specific direction. Conductive metal plates at the edges of the cell collect these flowing electrons, creating direct current (DC) electricity.Power Conversion: Your home appliances and the electrical grid run on alternating current (AC). To make the energy usable, the DC electricity is sent to a device called an inverter, which converts it into AC power.Usage & Storage: Once the power passes through the inverter, it is directed to your home's breaker box to run your appliances. Excess energy can be stored in a battery for later use, or sent back into the electrical grid to earn credits on your utility bill. Regalgrid +4For a quick, visual breakdown of the atomic process behind solar energy:54sHow does it work?
